title: DCSHADOW

headings.text: a. Compromise an Account with Administrative Permissions
b. Mimic a Domain Controller and Inject Malicious Changes
c. Leverage Injected Changes for Further Objectives

overview: DCShadow is a post-exploitation technique in which an attacker abuses compromised replication permissions to mimic a domain controller. By doing so, the adversary can push malicious changes into Active Directory (AD) with minimal logging, allowing for stealthy persistence, privilege escalation, or manipulation of AD data.

study.overview: DCShadow is a post-exploitation technique in which an attacker abuses compromised replication permissions to mimic a domain controller. By doing so, the adversary can push malicious changes into Active Directory (AD) with minimal logging, allowing for stealthy persistence, privilege escalation, or manipulation of AD data.
study.whySoc: Use this note as an analyst reference: understand the concept, identify the relevant telemetry, pivot through evidence, and document the decision path clearly.
keyConcepts: a. Compromise an Account with Administrative Permissions
b. Mimic a Domain Controller and Inject Malicious Changes
c. Leverage Injected Changes for Further Objectives
workflow: Identify the user, host, process, network flow, or identity object involved.
Collect the highest-value logs first and preserve timestamps and assumptions.
Pivot through related notes, telemetry sources, and MITRE references.
Document findings, gaps, containment options, and detection improvements.

splunkExamples.code:
index=wineventlog EventCode=4662
(ObjectType="replication" OR ObjectType="DRSUAPI")
NOT SubjectUserName="*$"
| table _time, ComputerName, SubjectUserName, ObjectType, AccessMask

index=wineventlog EventCode IN (5136,5141)
ObjectClass="nTDSDSA" OR ObjectClass="server"
| table _time, ComputerName, SubjectUserName, ObjectClass, OperationType
